features.summary: Microsoft reported record Q3 FY25 results with revenue of $70.1 billion (up 13%) and EPS of $3.46 (up 18%). Microsoft Cloud revenue reached $42.4 billion, growing 22% in constant currency. Azure revenue grew 33% with 16 points from AI services, though the quarter's outperformance came primarily from non-AI services acceleration in enterprise segments. The company processed over 100 trillion tokens (up 5x YoY) and has 15 million GitHub Copilot users (up 4x YoY). Commercial remaining performance obligation increased to $315 billion, up 34% YoY.
features.management_outlook: Management expects Q4 revenue growth to continue with Azure growing 34-35% in constant currency, though AI capacity constraints are expected beyond June despite bringing datacenter capacity online faster than planned. Microsoft Cloud gross margin is projected at roughly 67% for Q4, down from current levels due to AI infrastructure scaling. The company maintains its FY26 capex guidance, expecting growth at a lower rate than FY25 with a greater mix of short-lived assets more directly correlated to revenue.
features.risks: The primary risk is AI capacity constraints beyond June as demand is growing faster than datacenter capacity can be brought online, potentially limiting revenue growth in AI services. There's ongoing pressure on gross margins from scaling AI infrastructure investments. Windows OEM business faces uncertainty from elevated inventory levels that need to normalize. The company also faces normal quarterly variability in large Azure contract timing and potential macroeconomic headwinds affecting customer spending patterns.
features.key_drivers: AI services contributed 16 percentage points to Azure growth with strong demand across inference and training workloads, while non-AI Azure services showed accelerated growth in enterprise segments. Microsoft 365 Copilot adoption expanded across customer segments with growing ARPU from E5 and Copilot subscriptions. The company's integrated AI platform strategy is driving consumption across the full stack from infrastructure to applications, with customers like OpenAI representing both AI revenue and broader Azure consumption including databases and compute services.
